value: Headline: The crude story remains a tale of two narratives — a structurally tight US barrel against a building macro glut thesis, with WTI settling Tuesday at $72.68, down $1.18 on the session as US-Iran peace optimism deflated the geopolitical risk premium. Last week's WPSR (week ending June 12, 2026) delivered an 8.3 mb crude draw and pulled Cushing to just 20.0 mb — already below the ~25 mb operational floor — yet prices are bleeding lower as traders fixate on the prospect of returning Gulf barrels. The near-term physical tightness and the forward bearish glut call are pulling the curve in opposite directions, and that tension is the trade this morning. Crude & products: Per the latest weekly EIA report for the week ending June 12, 2026, commercial crude drew 8.3 mb to 418.2 mb, with the draw front-loaded in PADD II (-4.5 mb) and PADD III (-4.8 mb) as refiners ran flat-out. Refinery utilization climbed 1.4 points to 96.7%, lifting crude inputs to 17.19 mb/d, while the export pace eased to 4.87 mb/d from 5.57 mb/d. Cushing fell another 1.6 mb to 20.0 mb — operating below tank-bottom minimums and down 5.8 mb over five weeks from 25.82 mb on May 15, a hub-level stress that should be underpinning prompt WTI even as flat price sags. On products, gasoline drew 0.9 mb to 214.2 mb and jet fell 1.1 mb to 45.0 mb, while distillates built a modest 1.0 mb to 103.1 mb. Refining margins: Margins remain robust and are doing the work to justify that 96.7% run rate. The 3-2-1 crack sits at $50.12, with the distillate crack the standout at $57.75 and gasoline at $46.31 — strong enough to keep every available unit online. ULSD settled at $3.1055/gal, up 5 cents, bucking the crude-led selloff, while RBOB eased 5 cents to $2.833/gal. The distillate strength is no accident given the diesel-supply headlines out of Russia (see below), and it argues for refiners to keep maximizing middle-distillate yield through the back half of summer. Curve structure: WTI holds a backwardated M1-M12 spread of $4.29 and a prompt M1-M2 of $0.63, consistent with the sub-floor Cushing picture — physical tightness pulling the front of the board. Brent backwardation is shallower at $3.24 M1-M12 and just $0.19 M1-M2, reflecting the looser international balance the IEA glut call is flagging. The Brent-WTI spread sits narrow at $3.85, which weakens the US export arb — barrels stay home and pressure Cushing-area builds, though the offsetting peak-utilization draws have so far masked that dynamic. Product curves remain steeply backward ($16.56/bbl RBOB, $15.99/bbl ULSD M1-M12), the signature of front-end product tightness. News & geopolitics: The dominant driver is the unwinding Hormuz risk premium: the US Energy Secretary called Strait of Hormuz flows 'back towards normal,' and reports that Washington has lifted Iran oil sanctions amid stalling-but-alive peace talks have markets pricing in returning Gulf supply — the proximate cause of Tuesday's $1.18 WTI drop. The IEA piled on, warning the market is headed for a massive glut by 2027 as Gulf supply returns, though traders are rightly questioning how much Iranian oil can realistically come back near-term. Cutting the other way, Novak floated that Russia is considering a complete ban on diesel exports — directly supportive of the firm ULSD crack and the steep distillate backwardation. Meanwhile the NYT notes US crude is skipping the chance to grab Gulf market share, consistent with that narrow Brent-WTI arb and easing export pace, while tanker owners book their best week of the Hormuz crisis on elevated freight. Net: the geopolitical premium is deflating faster than the physical tightness is resolving, leaving prompt WTI caught between a sub-floor Cushing and a bearish forward macro tape.
